planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram TikTok Video Downloader Bot (grammY + Node.js ESM) — Production Plan

Summary:
A Telegram bot that accepts TikTok links and returns downloadable video files (preferably without watermark when available) by calling a RapidAPI TikTok download endpoint. The bot prioritizes fast responses, clear user feedback, and safe handling of Telegram upload constraints.

The service runs as a single Node.js process using grammY. MongoDB is used for basic persistence (users, usage limits, jobs, and audit logs). The bot can optionally store downloaded videos in object storage or simple filesystem storage for short-term caching and re-sending.

The design focuses on: 1) link parsing and validation, 2) resilient integration with RapidAPI, 3) a job-like workflow to handle long downloads and Telegram upload latency without blocking, 4) rate limiting and abuse prevention, and 5) observability and operational controls for production.

Product goals:
Primary goal: allow a user to send a TikTok URL (or share a TikTok post into the chat) and receive a downloadable video back in Telegram.

User experience goals:
1) Minimal friction: user pastes a link, bot responds with progress updates and delivers the file.
2) Multiple output formats when possible: video file, alternative download link, or audio-only (optional).
3) Predictable behavior: clear error messages for private/deleted videos, unsupported links, oversized files, and provider rate limits.

Business and operational goals:
1) Keep RapidAPI usage under control via quotas, per-user limits, and caching.
2) Maintain compliance and safety: avoid storing more data than necessary, implement basic abuse protections, and provide a way to delete user data.
3) Production reliability: retries, timeouts, and graceful degradation when external APIs are slow or failing.

High-level architecture:
High-level components in a single Node.js service:
1) Telegram Bot Layer (grammY)
   1) Receives messages, commands, and callback queries.
   2) Normalizes input (extract URL from message text, entities, forwarded messages).
   3) Sends progress updates and final results.

2) TikTok Download Orchestrator (internal service module)
   1) Validates TikTok URLs and extracts canonical form.
   2) Coordinates the download process: call RapidAPI, pick best media URL/variant, download binary, and prepare for Telegram upload.
   3) Implements fallback strategies and error mapping to user-friendly messages.

3) RapidAPI Client
   1) Encapsulates HTTP calls, headers, authentication, and request signing as required.
   2) Applies strict timeouts, retries with backoff for transient failures, and circuit-breaker-like behavior (soft-disable or degrade when failing repeatedly).

4) Media Fetcher + Storage Adapter
   1) Downloads the video file from a direct media URL returned by RapidAPI.
   2) Stores temporarily either in simple filesystem storage (ephemeral) or object storage (if needed for caching/retry).
   3) Computes file size, MIME type, duration (optional), and a content hash for deduplication.

5) Job/State Manager (in-process)
   1) Maintains per-chat/per-user active jobs in memory to prevent duplicate parallel downloads for the same user and to avoid spamming.
   2) Uses MongoDB for persistent job records so you can trace failures and handle restarts gracefully (best-effort).

6) Data Layer (MongoDB)
   1) Stores users, requests, usage counters, and job logs.
   2) Stores cached results metadata (not necessarily the full video) keyed by TikTok URL hash.

Execution model:
1) Bot receives a TikTok URL.
2) Create a job record and immediate user acknowledgement.
3) Call RapidAPI to obtain downloadable media URLs and metadata.
4) Select best candidate variant based on size constraints and user preferences.
5) Download media file, then upload to Telegram as a video/document.
6) Update job status, cache metadata, and send completion message.

Telegram constraints handling:
1) If the file exceeds Telegram upload limits for the bot, return a direct download link (from the upstream URL) rather than uploading the binary.
2) If the upstream is slow, send periodic status updates and allow cancel.

Security boundaries:
1) Never trust user-provided URLs; only allow TikTok domains and known short-link domains that resolve to TikTok.
2) Do not log secrets; keep RapidAPI key in environment variables.
3) Sanitize filenames and prevent path traversal if using filesystem storage.

Data model:
MongoDB collections (minimal but production-friendly):

1) users
1) telegramUserId (unique)
2) username, firstName, languageCode (optional)
3) createdAt, lastSeenAt
4) role: user/admin
5) preferences: defaultFormat (video/document), sendAs (video/document), watermarkPreference (best-effort)

2) downloadjobs
1) jobId (unique)
2) telegramChatId, telegramUserId
3) inputUrlOriginal
4) inputUrlCanonical
5) status: queued/inprogress/succeeded/failed/canceled
6) stage: validating/fetchingmetadata/downloading/uploading
7) provider: rapidapitiktokapi23
8) providerRequestId (if available)
9) output:
   1) selectedVariant (e.g., noWatermark/watermark/hd/sd)
   2) telegramFileId (if uploaded)
   3) fileSizeBytes, mimeType, durationSec (optional)
   4) cachedObjectKey or localPath (optional)
10) error:
   1) code (e.g., PROVIDERRATELIMIT, PRIVATEVIDEO, NETWORKTIMEOUT)
   2) message (safe, user-friendly)
   3) details (internal, not shown to user)
11) timestamps: createdAt, updatedAt, completedAt

3) cacheentries
1) urlHash (unique, derived from canonical URL)
2) inputUrlCanonical
3) providerResponseSummary (sanitized)
4) bestVariant:
   1) directUrl (if safe to store) or reference
   2) telegramFileId (preferred caching path)
   3) fileSizeBytes
5) expiresAt (TTL index recommended)
6) createdAt

4) usagecounters (optional if you prefer computing from logs)
1) telegramUserId
2) day (YYYY-MM-DD)
3) countRequests
4) countSuccess
5) rapidApiCalls

5) auditlogs (optional)
1) timestamp
2) type: command/message/error/adminaction
3) telegramUserId/chatId
4) payloadSummary (sanitized)

In-memory maps (process-local):
1) activeJobsByUserId to prevent multiple concurrent downloads per user (configurable).
2) simple rate limit buckets for burst control (complements MongoDB quotas).

Data retention:
1) Cache entries should expire quickly (hours/days) to reduce storage cost and risk.
2) Job logs can be retained longer for debugging (days/weeks) with optional pruning.

Key flows:
1) Basic download via message
1) User sends a TikTok URL.
2) Bot extracts URL, validates domain and format, and normalizes it.
3) Bot checks rate limits and whether the user already has an active job.
4) Bot responds immediately: “Working on it…” and creates a download job.
5) Bot checks cache by canonical URL hash:
   1) If cached Telegram fileId exists, re-send instantly (fast path).
   2) Otherwise proceed to provider.
6) Bot calls RapidAPI endpoint to retrieve download options and metadata.
7) Bot chooses a variant:
   1) Prefer no-watermark if provided.
   2) Otherwise best quality under Telegram upload limits.
   3) If unknown size, attempt HEAD/range or stream with a max cap.
8) Bot downloads the media file to temporary storage (or streams into Telegram upload if feasible).
9) Bot uploads to Telegram:
   1) Send as video when possible (better UX inline).
   2) Fall back to document for compatibility.
10) Bot updates job as succeeded, stores telegramFileId in cache, and confirms to user.

2) Oversized or blocked uploads
1) If file size exceeds Telegram limits or upload fails due to size.
2) Bot returns a message with:
   1) The direct download link (if stable) and a warning.
   2) Optional suggestion to use /audio or a lower-quality option if available.
3) Mark job as succeededwithlink (or succeeded but no telegramFileId).

3) Provider errors and retries
1) For transient errors (timeouts, 5xx), retry with exponential backoff up to a small limit.
2) For rate limiting, pause retries and present a friendly message: “Service is busy, try again later.”
3) For permanent errors:
   1) Private video: instruct user they must provide a public link.
   2) Removed/invalid: ask to double-check the URL.
4) Job is marked failed with a mapped error code.

4) Cancel flow
1) User issues /cancel while a job is in progress.
2) Bot marks job canceled and attempts to abort HTTP download and stop further processing.
3) Bot cleans up temporary file if created.

5) Admin and operations
1) Admin can check service health, current job counts, and recent error types.
2) Admin can block abusive users and adjust limits.
3) Admin can broadcast messages to users (optional).

6) Caching and deduplication
1) When a URL is processed, compute urlHash.
2) If a video is already uploaded to Telegram, reuse telegramFileId for instant resend.
3) If object storage/filesystem is used, reuse stored artifact when Telegram upload fails and a retry is needed.
4) Cache TTL ensures old content is removed automatically.

7) URL parsing details
1) Accept:
   1) www.tiktok.com/@user/video/ID
   2) vm.tiktok.com short links (resolve to canonical form)
2) Reject:
   1) Non-TikTok domains
   2) URLs with suspicious redirects or embedded credentials
3) Normalize by stripping tracking query params when safe and ensuring consistent encoding.

Tech stack:
Runtime and bot framework:
1) Node.js (ESM) single process service.
2) grammY for Telegram bot handling.
3) Long polling or webhook can be used; choose one based on deployment needs. Webhook is preferred for efficiency, but long polling is acceptable for simpler operations.

Persistence:
1) MongoDB for users, jobs, cache metadata, and rate-limit counters.
2) In-memory maps for active job locks and short-lived rate limiting to reduce database load.

External integration:
1) RapidAPI HTTP integration using a standard Node.js HTTP client.
2) RapidAPI credentials stored in environment variables (never hard-coded).

Storage:
1) Temporary downloads stored either in simple filesystem storage (ephemeral) or object storage (if you want cross-restart durability and better caching).
2) Store only what is necessary; prefer caching Telegram fileId to avoid re-upload.

Configuration and secrets:
1) TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN from environment variables.
2) RAPIDAPIKEY and RAPIDAPIHOST from environment variables.
3) Configurable limits: max concurrent jobs per user, daily request quotas, max download size, cache TTL.

Deployment:
1) A single Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Basic process supervision and automated restarts.

No additional databases or infrastructure beyond MongoDB and optional object storage/filesystem storage.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability and resilience:
1) Timeouts everywhere: provider request timeout, media download timeout, Telegram upload timeout.
2) Controlled retries: small retry budget with exponential backoff for transient failures.
3) Graceful degradation: if upload fails, provide a direct link; if provider fails, give a clear status.
4) Cleanup: always delete temporary files and cancel HTTP streams on abort.

Performance:
1) Fast path via Telegram fileId cache to avoid re-downloading and re-uploading.
2) Limit concurrency globally and per-user to keep memory and bandwidth stable.
3) Avoid loading large files fully in memory; prefer streaming or chunked download to disk.

Security:
1) Validate and normalize URLs; prevent SSRF by restricting domains and limiting redirects.
2) Protect secrets in environment variables and avoid logging them.
3) Rate limiting and abuse prevention:
   1) Per-user and per-chat quotas.
   2) Burst limits and cooldown periods.
   3) Optional blocklist.

Privacy and compliance:
1) Store minimal user data (Telegram IDs and basic metadata).
2) Provide /deleteme to remove user record and associated history (as feasible).
3) Cache TTL to reduce retained content.

Observability:
1) Structured logs for each job with jobId correlation.
2) Metrics (even if only logged): job counts, success rate, provider latency, upload latency, error categories.
3) Admin commands for quick operational insight.

Maintainability:
1) Separate modules: bot handlers, provider client, download orchestrator, storage adapter, data access.
2) Clear error taxonomy and mapping to user-facing messages.
3) Configuration-driven behavior for limits and caching.

Bot commands:
• /start — Introduce the bot and explain how to use it. (usage: /start) [user]
• /help — Show supported link formats, limits, and troubleshooting tips. (usage: /help) [user]
• /download — Download a TikTok video from a provided URL (useful when URL parsing from text fails). (usage: /download <tiktokurl>) [user]
• /audio — Attempt to extract and send audio-only when available (optional feature). (usage: /audio <tiktokurl>) [user]
• /cancel — Cancel the currently running download job for the user. (usage: /cancel) [user]
• /status — Show the status of your latest job (useful if Telegram delivery is delayed). (usage: /status) [user]
• /privacy — Explain what data is stored and for how long. (usage: /privacy) [user]
• /deleteme — Delete your stored user data and recent job history (best-effort). (usage: /deleteme) [user]
• /adminstats — Show recent job counts, success rate, and top error categories. (usage: /adminstats) [admin]
• /adminlimits — View or adjust configured limits (quotas, concurrency) via admin-only controls (implementation-defined). (usage: /adminlimits) [admin]
• /adminblock — Block a user from using the bot (abuse prevention). (usage: /adminblock <telegramUserId>) [admin]
• /adminunblock — Remove a user from the blocklist. (usage: /adminunblock <telegramUserId>) [admin]

RAPIDAPI_HOST=tiktok-api23.p.rapidapi.com

MONGODB_URI=

# Comma-separated Telegram numeric user IDs. Example: 123456789,987654321
ADMIN_TELEGRAM_USER_IDS=

# Logging (optional)
# info | warn | error
LOG_LEVEL=info

DOCS.md.content:
What this bot does

TikTok Grabber is a Telegram bot that takes a TikTok link and returns a downloadable video. It uses a RapidAPI endpoint to get the best download URL (no-watermark when available) and then sends the video back in Telegram.

Public commands

1) /start
What it does: Welcome message and quick examples.
Usage: /start

2) /help
What it does: Detailed usage, supported URLs, common errors, and privacy note.
Usage: /help

3) /dl <tiktok_url>
What it does: Downloads a TikTok video from the provided URL.
Usage: /dl https://www.tiktok.com/@user/video/1234567890
Notes: You can also just paste a TikTok link (same behavior).

4) /stats
What it does: Admin-only stats (requires ADMIN_TELEGRAM_USER_IDS).
Usage: /stats

Supported URLs
1) https://www.tiktok.com/@user/video/<id>
2) https://vm.tiktok.com/<short>
3) https://tiktok.com/t/<short>

Common errors
1) Missing or invalid URL
- Use: /dl <tiktok_url>

2) Private / deleted / region-blocked videos
- The provider may not be able to fetch these.

3) Telegram cannot send the video
- The file may be too large or Telegram may not be able to fetch it from the returned URL.
- The bot will fall back to sending a direct download link.

Privacy
The bot only uses the TikTok link you provide to fetch the downloadable file. If MongoDB is enabled, it stores a lightweight log entry (hash of the URL, userId, chatId, timestamp) and global counters.

Environment variables
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN (required): Telegram bot token
2) RAPIDAPI_KEY (required to download): RapidAPI key
3) RAPIDAPI_HOST (optional): defaults to tiktok-api23.p.rapidapi.com
4) MONGODB_URI (optional): enables persistence and metrics
5) ADMIN_TELEGRAM_USER_IDS (optional): comma-separated numeric Telegram user IDs for /stats
6) LOG_LEVEL (optional): info|warn|error (default info)

Run instructions
1) Install: npm run install:root
2) Start dev: npm run dev
3) Start prod: npm start

README.md.content:
TikTok Grabber Bot

A Telegram bot that downloads TikTok videos using a RapidAPI endpoint and sends them back to the chat.

Features
1) /dl <tiktok_url> downloads a TikTok video and sends it as a Telegram video (or document fallback)
2) Paste a TikTok link directly (same behavior as /dl)
3) Per-user cooldown (default 1 request per 20 seconds)
4) Optional MongoDB persistence for request logs and global metrics (runs fine without Mongo)
5) Optional admin-only /stats command

Architecture
1) src/index.js boots the service, checks env, connects DB (optional), starts long polling
2) src/bot.js wires grammY commands and message handlers
3) src/services/tiktokApi.js calls RapidAPI and extracts the best download URL
4) src/lib/storage.js is MongoDB helper and collections access (optional)
5) src/lib/rateLimit.js is per-user cooldown tracking
6) src/lib/extractUrl.js finds TikTok links in text
7) src/lib/errors.js normalizes errors for logs

Setup
1) Requirements
- Node.js 18+
- A Telegram bot token from @BotFather
- A RapidAPI key for the TikTok API endpoint
- Optional: MongoDB connection string

2) Install
- npm run install:root

3) Configure environment
- Copy .env.sample to .env and fill values

4) Run locally
- npm run dev

5) Run in production
- npm run build
- npm start

Commands
/start
- Welcome message and examples

/help
- Detailed usage, supported URLs, common errors, privacy note

/dl <tiktok_url>
- Downloads the TikTok video
Example: /dl https://www.tiktok.com/@taylorswift/video/7558098574555254046

/stats
- Admin-only. Shows global download and error metrics.

Environment variables
Required
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN: Telegram bot token
2) RAPIDAPI_KEY: RapidAPI key used to call the download endpoint

Optional
1) RAPIDAPI_HOST: defaults to tiktok-api23.p.rapidapi.com
2) MONGODB_URI: enables persistence and /stats metrics storage
3) ADMIN_TELEGRAM_USER_IDS: comma-separated Telegram numeric user IDs allowed to use /stats
4) LOG_LEVEL: defaults to info

Database
If MONGODB_URI is set:
1) downloads collection: { userId, chatId, urlHash, createdAt }
2) metrics collection: { _id:"global", totalDownloads, totalErrors, lastErrorAt, lastErrorMessage, createdAt, updatedAt }

Troubleshooting
1) Bot does not start
- Ensure TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is set

2) /dl fails with provider errors
- Ensure RAPIDAPI_KEY is set and valid
- The TikTok link might be private, deleted, or region-blocked

3) Telegram fails sending the video
- The file may be too large or Telegram cannot fetch the URL
- The bot will fall back to sending a direct download link

Extending
1) Add new commands under src/commands/*.js
2) They auto-register via src/commands/loader.js
3) Update src/index.js setMyCommands list to keep Telegram UI in sync

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram TikTok Video Downloader Bot (grammY + Node.js ESM) — Production Plan

Summary:
A Telegram bot that accepts TikTok links and returns downloadable video files (preferably without watermark when available) by calling a RapidAPI TikTok download endpoint. The bot prioritizes fast responses, clear user feedback, and safe handling of Telegram upload constraints.

The service runs as a single Node.js process using grammY. MongoDB is used for basic persistence (users, usage limits, jobs, and audit logs). The bot can optionally store downloaded videos in object storage or simple filesystem storage for short-term caching and re-sending.

The design focuses on: 1) link parsing and validation, 2) resilient integration with RapidAPI, 3) a job-like workflow to handle long downloads and Telegram upload latency without blocking, 4) rate limiting and abuse prevention, and 5) observability and operational controls for production.

Product goals:
Primary goal: allow a user to send a TikTok URL (or share a TikTok post into the chat) and receive a downloadable video back in Telegram.

User experience goals:
1) Minimal friction: user pastes a link, bot responds with progress updates and delivers the file.
2) Multiple output formats when possible: video file, alternative download link, or audio-only (optional).
3) Predictable behavior: clear error messages for private/deleted videos, unsupported links, oversized files, and provider rate limits.

Business and operational goals:
1) Keep RapidAPI usage under control via quotas, per-user limits, and caching.
2) Maintain compliance and safety: avoid storing more data than necessary, implement basic abuse protections, and provide a way to delete user data.
3) Production reliability: retries, timeouts, and graceful degradation when external APIs are slow or failing.

High-level architecture:
High-level components in a single Node.js service:
1) Telegram Bot Layer (grammY)
   1) Receives messages, commands, and callback queries.
   2) Normalizes input (extract URL from message text, entities, forwarded messages).
   3) Sends progress updates and final results.

2) TikTok Download Orchestrator (internal service module)
   1) Validates TikTok URLs and extracts canonical form.
   2) Coordinates the download process: call RapidAPI, pick best media URL/variant, download binary, and prepare for Telegram upload.
   3) Implements fallback strategies and error mapping to user-friendly messages.

3) RapidAPI Client
   1) Encapsulates HTTP calls, headers, authentication, and request signing as required.
   2) Applies strict timeouts, retries with backoff for transient failures, and circuit-breaker-like behavior (soft-disable or degrade when failing repeatedly).

4) Media Fetcher + Storage Adapter
   1) Downloads the video file from a direct media URL returned by RapidAPI.
   2) Stores temporarily either in simple filesystem storage (ephemeral) or object storage (if needed for caching/retry).
   3) Computes file size, MIME type, duration (optional), and a content hash for deduplication.

5) Job/State Manager (in-process)
   1) Maintains per-chat/per-user active jobs in memory to prevent duplicate parallel downloads for the same user and to avoid spamming.
   2) Uses MongoDB for persistent job records so you can trace failures and handle restarts gracefully (best-effort).

6) Data Layer (MongoDB)
   1) Stores users, requests, usage counters, and job logs.
   2) Stores cached results metadata (not necessarily the full video) keyed by TikTok URL hash.

Execution model:
1) Bot receives a TikTok URL.
2) Create a job record and immediate user acknowledgement.
3) Call RapidAPI to obtain downloadable media URLs and metadata.
4) Select best candidate variant based on size constraints and user preferences.
5) Download media file, then upload to Telegram as a video/document.
6) Update job status, cache metadata, and send completion message.

Telegram constraints handling:
1) If the file exceeds Telegram upload limits for the bot, return a direct download link (from the upstream URL) rather than uploading the binary.
2) If the upstream is slow, send periodic status updates and allow cancel.

Security boundaries:
1) Never trust user-provided URLs; only allow TikTok domains and known short-link domains that resolve to TikTok.
2) Do not log secrets; keep RapidAPI key in environment variables.
3) Sanitize filenames and prevent path traversal if using filesystem storage.

Data model:
MongoDB collections (minimal but production-friendly):

1) users
1) telegramUserId (unique)
2) username, firstName, languageCode (optional)
3) createdAt, lastSeenAt
4) role: user/admin
5) preferences: defaultFormat (video/document), sendAs (video/document), watermarkPreference (best-effort)

2) downloadjobs
1) jobId (unique)
2) telegramChatId, telegramUserId
3) inputUrlOriginal
4) inputUrlCanonical
5) status: queued/inprogress/succeeded/failed/canceled
6) stage: validating/fetchingmetadata/downloading/uploading
7) provider: rapidapitiktokapi23
8) providerRequestId (if available)
9) output:
   1) selectedVariant (e.g., noWatermark/watermark/hd/sd)
   2) telegramFileId (if uploaded)
   3) fileSizeBytes, mimeType, durationSec (optional)
   4) cachedObjectKey or localPath (optional)
10) error:
   1) code (e.g., PROVIDERRATELIMIT, PRIVATEVIDEO, NETWORKTIMEOUT)
   2) message (safe, user-friendly)
   3) details (internal, not shown to user)
11) timestamps: createdAt, updatedAt, completedAt

3) cacheentries
1) urlHash (unique, derived from canonical URL)
2) inputUrlCanonical
3) providerResponseSummary (sanitized)
4) bestVariant:
   1) directUrl (if safe to store) or reference
   2) telegramFileId (preferred caching path)
   3) fileSizeBytes
5) expiresAt (TTL index recommended)
6) createdAt

4) usagecounters (optional if you prefer computing from logs)
1) telegramUserId
2) day (YYYY-MM-DD)
3) countRequests
4) countSuccess
5) rapidApiCalls

5) auditlogs (optional)
1) timestamp
2) type: command/message/error/adminaction
3) telegramUserId/chatId
4) payloadSummary (sanitized)

In-memory maps (process-local):
1) activeJobsByUserId to prevent multiple concurrent downloads per user (configurable).
2) simple rate limit buckets for burst control (complements MongoDB quotas).

Data retention:
1) Cache entries should expire quickly (hours/days) to reduce storage cost and risk.
2) Job logs can be retained longer for debugging (days/weeks) with optional pruning.

Key flows:
1) Basic download via message
1) User sends a TikTok URL.
2) Bot extracts URL, validates domain and format, and normalizes it.
3) Bot checks rate limits and whether the user already has an active job.
4) Bot responds immediately: “Working on it…” and creates a download job.
5) Bot checks cache by canonical URL hash:
   1) If cached Telegram fileId exists, re-send instantly (fast path).
   2) Otherwise proceed to provider.
6) Bot calls RapidAPI endpoint to retrieve download options and metadata.
7) Bot chooses a variant:
   1) Prefer no-watermark if provided.
   2) Otherwise best quality under Telegram upload limits.
   3) If unknown size, attempt HEAD/range or stream with a max cap.
8) Bot downloads the media file to temporary storage (or streams into Telegram upload if feasible).
9) Bot uploads to Telegram:
   1) Send as video when possible (better UX inline).
   2) Fall back to document for compatibility.
10) Bot updates job as succeeded, stores telegramFileId in cache, and confirms to user.

2) Oversized or blocked uploads
1) If file size exceeds Telegram limits or upload fails due to size.
2) Bot returns a message with:
   1) The direct download link (if stable) and a warning.
   2) Optional suggestion to use /audio or a lower-quality option if available.
3) Mark job as succeededwithlink (or succeeded but no telegramFileId).

3) Provider errors and retries
1) For transient errors (timeouts, 5xx), retry with exponential backoff up to a small limit.
2) For rate limiting, pause retries and present a friendly message: “Service is busy, try again later.”
3) For permanent errors:
   1) Private video: instruct user they must provide a public link.
   2) Removed/invalid: ask to double-check the URL.
4) Job is marked failed with a mapped error code.

4) Cancel flow
1) User issues /cancel while a job is in progress.
2) Bot marks job canceled and attempts to abort HTTP download and stop further processing.
3) Bot cleans up temporary file if created.

5) Admin and operations
1) Admin can check service health, current job counts, and recent error types.
2) Admin can block abusive users and adjust limits.
3) Admin can broadcast messages to users (optional).

6) Caching and deduplication
1) When a URL is processed, compute urlHash.
2) If a video is already uploaded to Telegram, reuse telegramFileId for instant resend.
3) If object storage/filesystem is used, reuse stored artifact when Telegram upload fails and a retry is needed.
4) Cache TTL ensures old content is removed automatically.

7) URL parsing details
1) Accept:
   1) www.tiktok.com/@user/video/ID
   2) vm.tiktok.com short links (resolve to canonical form)
2) Reject:
   1) Non-TikTok domains
   2) URLs with suspicious redirects or embedded credentials
3) Normalize by stripping tracking query params when safe and ensuring consistent encoding.

Tech stack:
Runtime and bot framework:
1) Node.js (ESM) single process service.
2) grammY for Telegram bot handling.
3) Long polling or webhook can be used; choose one based on deployment needs. Webhook is preferred for efficiency, but long polling is acceptable for simpler operations.

Persistence:
1) MongoDB for users, jobs, cache metadata, and rate-limit counters.
2) In-memory maps for active job locks and short-lived rate limiting to reduce database load.

External integration:
1) RapidAPI HTTP integration using a standard Node.js HTTP client.
2) RapidAPI credentials stored in environment variables (never hard-coded).

Storage:
1) Temporary downloads stored either in simple filesystem storage (ephemeral) or object storage (if you want cross-restart durability and better caching).
2) Store only what is necessary; prefer caching Telegram fileId to avoid re-upload.

Configuration and secrets:
1) TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN from environment variables.
2) RAPIDAPIKEY and RAPIDAPIHOST from environment variables.
3) Configurable limits: max concurrent jobs per user, daily request quotas, max download size, cache TTL.

Deployment:
1) A single Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Basic process supervision and automated restarts.

No additional databases or infrastructure beyond MongoDB and optional object storage/filesystem storage.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability and resilience:
1) Timeouts everywhere: provider request timeout, media download timeout, Telegram upload timeout.
2) Controlled retries: small retry budget with exponential backoff for transient failures.
3) Graceful degradation: if upload fails, provide a direct link; if provider fails, give a clear status.
4) Cleanup: always delete temporary files and cancel HTTP streams on abort.

Performance:
1) Fast path via Telegram fileId cache to avoid re-downloading and re-uploading.
2) Limit concurrency globally and per-user to keep memory and bandwidth stable.
3) Avoid loading large files fully in memory; prefer streaming or chunked download to disk.

Security:
1) Validate and normalize URLs; prevent SSRF by restricting domains and limiting redirects.
2) Protect secrets in environment variables and avoid logging them.
3) Rate limiting and abuse prevention:
   1) Per-user and per-chat quotas.
   2) Burst limits and cooldown periods.
   3) Optional blocklist.

Privacy and compliance:
1) Store minimal user data (Telegram IDs and basic metadata).
2) Provide /deleteme to remove user record and associated history (as feasible).
3) Cache TTL to reduce retained content.

Observability:
1) Structured logs for each job with jobId correlation.
2) Metrics (even if only logged): job counts, success rate, provider latency, upload latency, error categories.
3) Admin commands for quick operational insight.

Maintainability:
1) Separate modules: bot handlers, provider client, download orchestrator, storage adapter, data access.
2) Clear error taxonomy and mapping to user-facing messages.
3) Configuration-driven behavior for limits and caching.

Bot commands:
• /start — Introduce the bot and explain how to use it. (usage: /start) [user]
• /help — Show supported link formats, limits, and troubleshooting tips. (usage: /help) [user]
• /download — Download a TikTok video from a provided URL (useful when URL parsing from text fails). (usage: /download <tiktokurl>) [user]
• /audio — Attempt to extract and send audio-only when available (optional feature). (usage: /audio <tiktokurl>) [user]
• /cancel — Cancel the currently running download job for the user. (usage: /cancel) [user]
• /status — Show the status of your latest job (useful if Telegram delivery is delayed). (usage: /status) [user]
• /privacy — Explain what data is stored and for how long. (usage: /privacy) [user]
• /deleteme — Delete your stored user data and recent job history (best-effort). (usage: /deleteme) [user]
• /adminstats — Show recent job counts, success rate, and top error categories. (usage: /adminstats) [admin]
• /adminlimits — View or adjust configured limits (quotas, concurrency) via admin-only controls (implementation-defined). (usage: /adminlimits) [admin]
• /adminblock — Block a user from using the bot (abuse prevention). (usage: /adminblock <telegramUserId>) [admin]
• /adminunblock — Remove a user from the blocklist. (usage: /adminunblock <telegramUserId>) [admin]

7) Bot chooses a variant:
   1) Prefer no-watermark if provided.
   2) Otherwise best quality under Telegram upload limits.
   3) If unknown size, attempt HEAD/range or stream with a max cap.
8) Bot downloads the media file to temporary storage (or streams into Telegram upload if feasible).
9) Bot uploads to Telegram:
   1) Send as video when possible (better UX inline).
   2) Fall back to document for compatibility.
10) Bot updates job as succeeded, stores telegramFileId in cache, and confirms to user.

2) Oversized or blocked uploads
1) If file size exceeds Telegram limits or upload fails due to size.
2) Bot returns a message with:
   1) The direct download link (if stable) and a warning.
   2) Optional suggestion to use /audio or a lower-quality option if available.
3) Mark job as succeededwithlink (or succeeded but no telegramFileId).

3) Provider errors and retries
1) For transient errors (timeouts, 5xx), retry with exponential backoff up to a small limit.
2) For rate limiting, pause retries and present a friendly message: “Service is busy, try again later.”
3) For permanent errors:
   1) Private video: instruct user they must provide a public link.
   2) Removed/invalid: ask to double-check the URL.
4) Job is marked failed with a mapped error code.

4) Cancel flow
1) User issues /cancel while a job is in progress.
2) Bot marks job canceled and attempts to abort HTTP download and stop further processing.
3) Bot cleans up temporary file if created.

5) Admin and operations
1) Admin can check service health, current job counts, and recent error types.
2) Admin can block abusive users and adjust limits.
3) Admin can broadcast messages to users (optional).

6) Caching and deduplication
1) When a URL is processed, compute urlHash.
2) If a video is already uploaded to Telegram, reuse telegramFileId for instant resend.
3) If object storage/filesystem is used, reuse stored artifact when Telegram upload fails and a retry is needed.
4) Cache TTL ensures old content is removed automatically.

7) URL parsing details
1) Accept:
   1) www.tiktok.com/@user/video/ID
   2) vm.tiktok.com short links (resolve to canonical form)
2) Reject:
   1) Non-TikTok domains
   2) URLs with suspicious redirects or embedded credentials
3) Normalize by stripping tracking query params when safe and ensuring consistent encoding.

DOCS.md.content:
What this bot does

TikTok Grabber is a Telegram bot that takes a TikTok link and returns a downloadable video. It uses a RapidAPI endpoint to get the best download URL (no-watermark when available) and then sends the video back in Telegram.

Public commands

1) /start
What it does: Welcome message and quick examples.
Usage: /start

2) /help
What it does: Detailed usage, supported URLs, common errors, and privacy note.
Usage: /help

3) /dl <tiktok_url>
What it does: Downloads a TikTok video from the provided URL.
Usage: /dl https://www.tiktok.com/@user/video/1234567890
Notes: You can also just paste a TikTok link (same behavior).

4) /stats
What it does: Admin-only stats (requires ADMIN_TELEGRAM_USER_IDS).
Usage: /stats

Supported URLs
1) https://www.tiktok.com/@user/video/<id>
2) https://vm.tiktok.com/<short>
3) https://tiktok.com/t/<short>

Common errors
1) Missing or invalid URL
- Use: /dl <tiktok_url>

2) Private / deleted / region-blocked videos
- The provider may not be able to fetch these.

3) Telegram cannot send the video
- The file may be too large or Telegram may not be able to fetch it from the returned URL.
- The bot will fall back to sending a direct download link.

Privacy
The bot only uses the TikTok link you provide to fetch the downloadable file. If MongoDB is enabled, it stores a lightweight log entry (hash of the URL, userId, chatId, timestamp) and global counters.

Environment variables
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN (required): Telegram bot token
2) RAPIDAPI_KEY (required to download): RapidAPI key
3) RAPIDAPI_HOST (optional): defaults to tiktok-api23.p.rapidapi.com
4) MONGODB_URI (optional): enables persistence and metrics
5) ADMIN_TELEGRAM_USER_IDS (optional): comma-separated numeric Telegram user IDs for /stats
6) LOG_LEVEL (optional): info|warn|error (default info)

Run instructions
1) Install: npm run install:root
2) Start dev: npm run dev
3) Start prod: npm start

README.md.content:
TikTok Grabber Bot

A Telegram bot that downloads TikTok videos using a RapidAPI endpoint and sends them back to the chat.

Features
1) /dl <tiktok_url> downloads a TikTok video and sends it as a Telegram video (or document fallback)
2) Paste a TikTok link directly (same behavior as /dl)
3) Per-user cooldown (default 1 request per 20 seconds)
4) Optional MongoDB persistence for request logs and global metrics (runs fine without Mongo)
5) Optional admin-only /stats command

Architecture
1) src/index.js boots the service, checks env, connects DB (optional), starts long polling
2) src/bot.js wires grammY commands and message handlers
3) src/services/tiktokApi.js calls RapidAPI and extracts the best download URL
4) src/lib/storage.js is MongoDB helper and collections access (optional)
5) src/lib/rateLimit.js is per-user cooldown tracking
6) src/lib/extractUrl.js finds TikTok links in text
7) src/lib/errors.js normalizes errors for logs

Setup
1) Requirements
- Node.js 18+
- A Telegram bot token from @BotFather
- A RapidAPI key for the TikTok API endpoint
- Optional: MongoDB connection string

2) Install
- npm run install:root

3) Configure environment
- Copy .env.sample to .env and fill values

4) Run locally
- npm run dev

5) Run in production
- npm run build
- npm start

Commands
/start
- Welcome message and examples

/help
- Detailed usage, supported URLs, common errors, privacy note

/dl <tiktok_url>
- Downloads the TikTok video
Example: /dl https://www.tiktok.com/@taylorswift/video/7558098574555254046

/stats
- Admin-only. Shows global download and error metrics.

Environment variables
Required
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN: Telegram bot token
2) RAPIDAPI_KEY: RapidAPI key used to call the download endpoint

Optional
1) RAPIDAPI_HOST: defaults to tiktok-api23.p.rapidapi.com
2) MONGODB_URI: enables persistence and /stats metrics storage
3) ADMIN_TELEGRAM_USER_IDS: comma-separated Telegram numeric user IDs allowed to use /stats
4) LOG_LEVEL: defaults to info

Database
If MONGODB_URI is set:
1) downloads collection: { userId, chatId, urlHash, createdAt }
2) metrics collection: { _id:"global", totalDownloads, totalErrors, lastErrorAt, lastErrorMessage, createdAt, updatedAt }

Troubleshooting
1) Bot does not start
- Ensure TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is set

2) /dl fails with provider errors
- Ensure RAPIDAPI_KEY is set and valid
- The TikTok link might be private, deleted, or region-blocked

3) Telegram fails sending the video
- The file may be too large or Telegram cannot fetch the URL
- The bot will fall back to sending a direct download link

Extending
1) Add new commands under src/commands/*.js
2) They auto-register via src/commands/loader.js
3) Update src/index.js setMyCommands list to keep Telegram UI in sync

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram TikTok Video Downloader Bot (grammY + Node.js ESM) — Production Plan

Summary:
A Telegram bot that accepts TikTok links and returns downloadable video files (preferably without watermark when available) by calling a RapidAPI TikTok download endpoint. The bot prioritizes fast responses, clear user feedback, and safe handling of Telegram upload constraints.

The service runs as a single Node.js process using grammY. MongoDB is used for basic persistence (users, usage limits, jobs, and audit logs). The bot can optionally store downloaded videos in object storage or simple filesystem storage for short-term caching and re-sending.

The design focuses on: 1) link parsing and validation, 2) resilient integration with RapidAPI, 3) a job-like workflow to handle long downloads and Telegram upload latency without blocking, 4) rate limiting and abuse prevention, and 5) observability and operational controls for production.

Product goals:
Primary goal: allow a user to send a TikTok URL (or share a TikTok post into the chat) and receive a downloadable video back in Telegram.

User experience goals:
1) Minimal friction: user pastes a link, bot responds with progress updates and delivers the file.
2) Multiple output formats when possible: video file, alternative download link, or audio-only (optional).
3) Predictable behavior: clear error messages for private/deleted videos, unsupported links, oversized files, and provider rate limits.

Business and operational goals:
1) Keep RapidAPI usage under control via quotas, per-user limits, and caching.
2) Maintain compliance and safety: avoid storing more data than necessary, implement basic abuse protections, and provide a way to delete user data.
3) Production reliability: retries, timeouts, and graceful degradation when external APIs are slow or failing.

High-level architecture:
High-level components in a single Node.js service:
1) Telegram Bot Layer (grammY)
   1) Receives messages, commands, and callback queries.
   2) Normalizes input (extract URL from message text, entities, forwarded messages).
   3) Sends progress updates and final results.

2) TikTok Download Orchestrator (internal service module)
   1) Validates TikTok URLs and extracts canonical form.
   2) Coordinates the download process: call RapidAPI, pick best media URL/variant, download binary, and prepare for Telegram upload.
   3) Implements fallback strategies and error mapping to user-friendly messages.

3) RapidAPI Client
   1) Encapsulates HTTP calls, headers, authentication, and request signing as required.
   2) Applies strict timeouts, retries with backoff for transient failures, and circuit-breaker-like behavior (soft-disable or degrade when failing repeatedly).

4) Media Fetcher + Storage Adapter
   1) Downloads the video file from a direct media URL returned by RapidAPI.
   2) Stores temporarily either in simple filesystem storage (ephemeral) or object storage (if needed for caching/retry).
   3) Computes file size, MIME type, duration (optional), and a content hash for deduplication.

5) Job/State Manager (in-process)
   1) Maintains per-chat/per-user active jobs in memory to prevent duplicate parallel downloads for the same user and to avoid spamming.
   2) Uses MongoDB for persistent job records so you can trace failures and handle restarts gracefully (best-effort).

6) Data Layer (MongoDB)
   1) Stores users, requests, usage counters, and job logs.
   2) Stores cached results metadata (not necessarily the full video) keyed by TikTok URL hash.

Execution model:
1) Bot receives a TikTok URL.
2) Create a job record and immediate user acknowledgement.
3) Call RapidAPI to obtain downloadable media URLs and metadata.
4) Select best candidate variant based on size constraints and user preferences.
5) Download media file, then upload to Telegram as a video/document.
6) Update job status, cache metadata, and send completion message.

Telegram constraints handling:
1) If the file exceeds Telegram upload limits for the bot, return a direct download link (from the upstream URL) rather than uploading the binary.
2) If the upstream is slow, send periodic status updates and allow cancel.

Security boundaries:
1) Never trust user-provided URLs; only allow TikTok domains and known short-link domains that resolve to TikTok.
2) Do not log secrets; keep RapidAPI key in environment variables.
3) Sanitize filenames and prevent path traversal if using filesystem storage.

Data model:
MongoDB collections (minimal but production-friendly):

1) users
1) telegramUserId (unique)
2) username, firstName, languageCode (optional)
3) createdAt, lastSeenAt
4) role: user/admin
5) preferences: defaultFormat (video/document), sendAs (video/document), watermarkPreference (best-effort)

2) downloadjobs
1) jobId (unique)
2) telegramChatId, telegramUserId
3) inputUrlOriginal
4) inputUrlCanonical
5) status: queued/inprogress/succeeded/failed/canceled
6) stage: validating/fetchingmetadata/downloading/uploading
7) provider: rapidapitiktokapi23
8) providerRequestId (if available)
9) output:
   1) selectedVariant (e.g., noWatermark/watermark/hd/sd)
   2) telegramFileId (if uploaded)
   3) fileSizeBytes, mimeType, durationSec (optional)
   4) cachedObjectKey or localPath (optional)
10) error:
   1) code (e.g., PROVIDERRATELIMIT, PRIVATEVIDEO, NETWORKTIMEOUT)
   2) message (safe, user-friendly)
   3) details (internal, not shown to user)
11) timestamps: createdAt, updatedAt, completedAt

3) cacheentries
1) urlHash (unique, derived from canonical URL)
2) inputUrlCanonical
3) providerResponseSummary (sanitized)
4) bestVariant:
   1) directUrl (if safe to store) or reference
   2) telegramFileId (preferred caching path)
   3) fileSizeBytes
5) expiresAt (TTL index recommended)
6) createdAt

4) usagecounters (optional if you prefer computing from logs)
1) telegramUserId
2) day (YYYY-MM-DD)
3) countRequests
4) countSuccess
5) rapidApiCalls

5) auditlogs (optional)
1) timestamp
2) type: command/message/error/adminaction
3) telegramUserId/chatId
4) payloadSummary (sanitized)

In-memory maps (process-local):
1) activeJobsByUserId to prevent multiple concurrent downloads per user (configurable).
2) simple rate limit buckets for burst control (complements MongoDB quotas).

Data retention:
1) Cache entries should expire quickly (hours/days) to reduce storage cost and risk.
2) Job logs can be retained longer for debugging (days/weeks) with optional pruning.

1) Basic download via message
1) User sends a TikTok URL.
2) Bot extracts URL, validates domain and format, and normalizes it.
3) Bot checks rate limits and whether the user already has an active job.
4) Bot responds immediately: “Working on it…” and creates a download job.

   1) If cached Telegram fileId exists, re-send instantly (fast path).

Observability:
1) Structured logs for each job with jobId correlation.
2) Metrics (even if only logged): job counts, success rate, provider latency, upload latency, error categories.
3) Admin commands for quick operational insight.

Maintainability:
1) Separate modules: bot handlers, provider client, download orchestrator, storage adapter, data access.
2) Clear error taxonomy and mapping to user-facing messages.
3) Configuration-driven behavior for limits and caching.

Bot commands:
• /start — Introduce the bot and explain how to use it. (usage: /start) [user]
• /help — Show supported link formats, limits, and troubleshooting tips. (usage: /help) [user]
• /download — Download a TikTok video from a provided URL (useful when URL parsing from text fails). (usage: /download <tiktokurl>) [user]
• /audio — Attempt to extract and send audio-only when available (optional feature). (usage: /audio <tiktokurl>) [user]
• /cancel — Cancel the currently running download job for the user. (usage: /cancel) [user]
• /status — Show the status of your latest job (useful if Telegram delivery is delayed). (usage: /status) [user]
• /privacy — Explain what data is stored and for how long. (usage: /privacy) [user]
• /deleteme — Delete your stored user data and recent job history (best-effort). (usage: /deleteme) [user]
• /adminstats — Show recent job counts, success rate, and top error categories. (usage: /adminstats) [admin]
• /adminlimits — View or adjust configured limits (quotas, concurrency) via admin-only controls (implementation-defined). (usage: /adminlimits) [admin]
• /adminblock — Block a user from using the bot (abuse prevention). (usage: /adminblock <telegramUserId>) [admin]
• /adminunblock — Remove a user from the blocklist. (usage: /adminunblock <telegramUserId>) [admin]

planSections.productGoals:
Primary goal: allow a user to send a TikTok URL (or share a TikTok post into the chat) and receive a downloadable video back in Telegram.

User experience goals:
1) Minimal friction: user pastes a link, bot responds with progress updates and delivers the file.
2) Multiple output formats when possible: video file, alternative download link, or audio-only (optional).
3) Predictable behavior: clear error messages for private/deleted videos, unsupported links, oversized files, and provider rate limits.

Business and operational goals:
1) Keep RapidAPI usage under control via quotas, per-user limits, and caching.
2) Maintain compliance and safety: avoid storing more data than necessary, implement basic abuse protections, and provide a way to delete user data.
3) Production reliability: retries, timeouts, and graceful degradation when external APIs are slow or failing.
planSections.highLevelArchitecture:
High-level components in a single Node.js service:
1) Telegram Bot Layer (grammY)
   1) Receives messages, commands, and callback queries.
   2) Normalizes input (extract URL from message text, entities, forwarded messages).
   3) Sends progress updates and final results.

2) TikTok Download Orchestrator (internal service module)
   1) Validates TikTok URLs and extracts canonical form.
   2) Coordinates the download process: call RapidAPI, pick best media URL/variant, download binary, and prepare for Telegram upload.
   3) Implements fallback strategies and error mapping to user-friendly messages.

3) RapidAPI Client
   1) Encapsulates HTTP calls, headers, authentication, and request signing as required.
   2) Applies strict timeouts, retries with backoff for transient failures, and circuit-breaker-like behavior (soft-disable or degrade when failing repeatedly).

4) Media Fetcher + Storage Adapter
   1) Downloads the video file from a direct media URL returned by RapidAPI.
   2) Stores temporarily either in simple filesystem storage (ephemeral) or object storage (if needed for caching/retry).
   3) Computes file size, MIME type, duration (optional), and a content hash for deduplication.

5) Job/State Manager (in-process)
   1) Maintains per-chat/per-user active jobs in memory to prevent duplicate parallel downloads for the same user and to avoid spamming.
   2) Uses MongoDB for persistent job records so you can trace failures and handle restarts gracefully (best-effort).

6) Data Layer (MongoDB)
   1) Stores users, requests, usage counters, and job logs.
   2) Stores cached results metadata (not necessarily the full video) keyed by TikTok URL hash.

Execution model:
1) Bot receives a TikTok URL.
2) Create a job record and immediate user acknowledgement.
3) Call RapidAPI to obtain downloadable media URLs and metadata.
4) Select best candidate variant based on size constraints and user preferences.
5) Download media file, then upload to Telegram as a video/document.
6) Update job status, cache metadata, and send completion message.

Telegram constraints handling:
1) If the file exceeds Telegram upload limits for the bot, return a direct download link (from the upstream URL) rather than uploading the binary.
2) If the upstream is slow, send periodic status updates and allow cancel.

Security boundaries:
1) Never trust user-provided URLs; only allow TikTok domains and known short-link domains that resolve to TikTok.
2) Do not log secrets; keep RapidAPI key in environment variables.
3) Sanitize filenames and prevent path traversal if using filesystem storage.
planSections.dataModel:
MongoDB collections (minimal but production-friendly):

1) users
1) telegramUserId (unique)
2) username, firstName, languageCode (optional)
3) createdAt, lastSeenAt
4) role: user/admin
5) preferences: defaultFormat (video/document), sendAs (video/document), watermarkPreference (best-effort)

2) downloadjobs
1) jobId (unique)
2) telegramChatId, telegramUserId
3) inputUrlOriginal
4) inputUrlCanonical
5) status: queued/inprogress/succeeded/failed/canceled
6) stage: validating/fetchingmetadata/downloading/uploading
7) provider: rapidapitiktokapi23
8) providerRequestId (if available)
9) output:
   1) selectedVariant (e.g., noWatermark/watermark/hd/sd)
   2) telegramFileId (if uploaded)
   3) fileSizeBytes, mimeType, durationSec (optional)
   4) cachedObjectKey or localPath (optional)
10) error:
   1) code (e.g., PROVIDERRATELIMIT, PRIVATEVIDEO, NETWORKTIMEOUT)
   2) message (safe, user-friendly)
   3) details (internal, not shown to user)
11) timestamps: createdAt, updatedAt, completedAt

3) cacheentries
1) urlHash (unique, derived from canonical URL)
2) inputUrlCanonical
3) providerResponseSummary (sanitized)
4) bestVariant:
   1) directUrl (if safe to store) or reference
   2) telegramFileId (preferred caching path)
   3) fileSizeBytes
5) expiresAt (TTL index recommended)
6) createdAt

4) usagecounters (optional if you prefer computing from logs)
1) telegramUserId
2) day (YYYY-MM-DD)
3) countRequests
4) countSuccess
5) rapidApiCalls

5) auditlogs (optional)
1) timestamp
2) type: command/message/error/adminaction
3) telegramUserId/chatId
4) payloadSummary (sanitized)

In-memory maps (process-local):
1) activeJobsByUserId to prevent multiple concurrent downloads per user (configurable).
2) simple rate limit buckets for burst control (complements MongoDB quotas).

Data retention:
1) Cache entries should expire quickly (hours/days) to reduce storage cost and risk.
2) Job logs can be retained longer for debugging (days/weeks) with optional pruning.
planSections.keyFlows:
1) Basic download via message
1) User sends a TikTok URL.
2) Bot extracts URL, validates domain and format, and normalizes it.
3) Bot checks rate limits and whether the user already has an active job.
4) Bot responds immediately: “Working on it…” and creates a download job.
5) Bot checks cache by canonical URL hash:
   1) If cached Telegram fileId exists, re-send instantly (fast path).
   2) Otherwise proceed to provider.
6) Bot calls RapidAPI endpoint to retrieve download options and metadata.
7) Bot chooses a variant:
   1) Prefer no-watermark if provided.
   2) Otherwise best quality under Telegram upload limits.
   3) If unknown size, attempt HEAD/range or stream with a max cap.
8) Bot downloads the media file to temporary storage (or streams into Telegram upload if feasible).
9) Bot uploads to Telegram:
   1) Send as video when possible (better UX inline).
   2) Fall back to document for compatibility.
10) Bot updates job as succeeded, stores telegramFileId in cache, and confirms to user.

2) Oversized or blocked uploads
1) If file size exceeds Telegram limits or upload fails due to size.
2) Bot returns a message with:
   1) The direct download link (if stable) and a warning.
   2) Optional suggestion to use /audio or a lower-quality option if available.
3) Mark job as succeededwithlink (or succeeded but no telegramFileId).

3) Provider errors and retries
1) For transient errors (timeouts, 5xx), retry with exponential backoff up to a small limit.
2) For rate limiting, pause retries and present a friendly message: “Service is busy, try again later.”
3) For permanent errors:
   1) Private video: instruct user they must provide a public link.
   2) Removed/invalid: ask to double-check the URL.
4) Job is marked failed with a mapped error code.

4) Cancel flow
1) User issues /cancel while a job is in progress.
2) Bot marks job canceled and attempts to abort HTTP download and stop further processing.
3) Bot cleans up temporary file if created.

5) Admin and operations
1) Admin can check service health, current job counts, and recent error types.
2) Admin can block abusive users and adjust limits.
3) Admin can broadcast messages to users (optional).

6) Caching and deduplication
1) When a URL is processed, compute urlHash.
2) If a video is already uploaded to Telegram, reuse telegramFileId for instant resend.
3) If object storage/filesystem is used, reuse stored artifact when Telegram upload fails and a retry is needed.
4) Cache TTL ensures old content is removed automatically.

7) URL parsing details
1) Accept:
   1) www.tiktok.com/@user/video/ID
   2) vm.tiktok.com short links (resolve to canonical form)
2) Reject:
   1) Non-TikTok domains
   2) URLs with suspicious redirects or embedded credentials
3) Normalize by stripping tracking query params when safe and ensuring consistent encoding.
planSections.techStack:
Runtime and bot framework:
1) Node.js (ESM) single process service.
2) grammY for Telegram bot handling.
3) Long polling or webhook can be used; choose one based on deployment needs. Webhook is preferred for efficiency, but long polling is acceptable for simpler operations.

Persistence:
1) MongoDB for users, jobs, cache metadata, and rate-limit counters.
2) In-memory maps for active job locks and short-lived rate limiting to reduce database load.

External integration:
1) RapidAPI HTTP integration using a standard Node.js HTTP client.
2) RapidAPI credentials stored in environment variables (never hard-coded).

Storage:
1) Temporary downloads stored either in simple filesystem storage (ephemeral) or object storage (if you want cross-restart durability and better caching).
2) Store only what is necessary; prefer caching Telegram fileId to avoid re-upload.

Configuration and secrets:
1) TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN from environment variables.
2) RAPIDAPIKEY and RAPIDAPIHOST from environment variables.
3) Configurable limits: max concurrent jobs per user, daily request quotas, max download size, cache TTL.

Deployment:
1) A single Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Basic process supervision and automated restarts.

No additional databases or infrastructure beyond MongoDB and optional object storage/filesystem storage.
planSections.nonFunctional:
Reliability and resilience:
1) Timeouts everywhere: provider request timeout, media download timeout, Telegram upload timeout.
2) Controlled retries: small retry budget with exponential backoff for transient failures.
3) Graceful degradation: if upload fails, provide a direct link; if provider fails, give a clear status.
4) Cleanup: always delete temporary files and cancel HTTP streams on abort.

Performance:
1) Fast path via Telegram fileId cache to avoid re-downloading and re-uploading.
2) Limit concurrency globally and per-user to keep memory and bandwidth stable.
3) Avoid loading large files fully in memory; prefer streaming or chunked download to disk.

Security:
1) Validate and normalize URLs; prevent SSRF by restricting domains and limiting redirects.
2) Protect secrets in environment variables and avoid logging them.
3) Rate limiting and abuse prevention:
   1) Per-user and per-chat quotas.
   2) Burst limits and cooldown periods.
   3) Optional blocklist.

Privacy and compliance:
1) Store minimal user data (Telegram IDs and basic metadata).
2) Provide /deleteme to remove user record and associated history (as feasible).
3) Cache TTL to reduce retained content.

Observability:
1) Structured logs for each job with jobId correlation.
2) Metrics (even if only logged): job counts, success rate, provider latency, upload latency, error categories.
3) Admin commands for quick operational insight.

Maintainability:
1) Separate modules: bot handlers, provider client, download orchestrator, storage adapter, data access.
2) Clear error taxonomy and mapping to user-facing messages.
3) Configuration-driven behavior for limits and caching.

envVarDocs.title: Telegram Bot Token
envVarDocs.description: Token for your Telegram bot from BotFather. Required to start the bot.
envVarDocs.usage: Read on boot; the process exits if missing.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Create a bot with @BotFather and copy the token.

envVarDocs.title: RapidAPI Key
envVarDocs.description: API key used to call the TikTok download endpoint via RapidAPI.
envVarDocs.usage: Sent as the x-rapidapi-key header on every download request.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Get it from your RapidAPI account for the TikTok API you subscribed to.

envVarDocs.title: RapidAPI Host
envVarDocs.description: RapidAPI host header for the TikTok endpoint.
envVarDocs.usage: Sent as x-rapidapi-host. Defaults to tiktok-api23.p.rapidapi.com when not set.
envVarDocs.howToGet: From the RapidAPI endpoint page (Host header value).

envVarDocs.title: MongoDB Connection String
envVarDocs.description: Optional MongoDB connection string used for request logs and global metrics.
envVarDocs.usage: If missing, DB features are disabled and the bot runs in-memory only.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Create a MongoDB database (MongoDB Atlas or self-hosted) and copy the connection URI.

envVarDocs.title: Admin Telegram User IDs
envVarDocs.description: Comma-separated numeric Telegram user IDs allowed to use /stats.
envVarDocs.usage: If missing or empty, no one is admin (safe default).
envVarDocs.howToGet: Get your numeric Telegram user ID (e.g., via @userinfobot).

envVarDocs.title: Log Level
envVarDocs.description: Controls verbosity of logs (basic gating for future expansion).
